content.text: Stochastic Variational Inference (SVI) is an algorithm for fitting variational distributions in Bayesian models when the dataset is too large to process in full at each optimization step. Classical variational inference (VI) minimizes the KL divergence between a tractable approximating family q(z; λ) and the true posterior p(z | x) by maximizing the Evidence Lower BOund (ELBO):

When N (the number of data points) is large, evaluating the ELBO requires summing over all N observations at each gradient step — prohibitively expensive. SVI solves this by showing that when latent variables factorize over data points, the full-data ELBO gradient can be
of size M by scaling the data-dependent terms by N/M. This enables optimization of the ELBO with SGD-style updates, making VI applicable to datasets with millions of observations.

The fundamental challenge in Bayesian deep learning is posterior inference: given observed data x, compute p(z | x) — the distribution over latent variables z given the observations. For any non-trivial generative model, this posterior is intractable to compute exactly. MCMC methods can approximate it, but they are extremely slow for large datasets — a Gibbs sampler over a million data points requires a full dataset scan per sample.

SVI unlocked Bayesian modeling at the scale of modern machine learning. Prior to SVI (Hoffman et al. 2013), applying VI to a topic model with a million documents was computationally infeasible. SVI made it routine. More broadly, SVI is the optimization backbone behind Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), beta-VAEs, VQ-VAEs, normalizing flows trained with ELBO objectives, and Gaussian process approximations. Virtually every scalable Bayesian deep generative model uses SVI or a closely related technique.

The concrete pain point SVI addresses: classical coordinate ascent VI for a Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) model with 10,000 documents takes hours per pass. SVI with mini-batches of 256 documents achieves the same quality in minutes, and can process streaming data online — updating the model as new documents arrive without revisiting old ones.
SVI reduces per-update cost from O(N) to O(M) where M << N. This is what makes Bayesian models applicable at internet scale.
Because each update uses only a mini-batch, SVI naturally supports streaming data — the model updates continuously as new observations arrive, without storing the full dataset.
SVI objective is a scalar (ELBO), so Adam, RMSProp, and other adaptive optimizers improve convergence versus vanilla SGD with no algorithmic changes.
In VAE-style models, an encoder network (amortized inference network) replaces per-datapoint local parameters, sharing computation across data points. SVI's mini-batch framework applies directly.
When mini-batches are drawn uniformly, the N/M-scaled gradient is an unbiased estimate of the full-data gradient — unlike some approximations that introduce systematic bias.
For exponential-family models, the natural gradient SVI update has a simple closed form equivalent to an EM step, converging faster than standard gradient SVI.
From Batch to Stochastic Variational Inference

Classical mean-field variational inference iterates over the entire dataset to compute each gradient update — unacceptably slow for large N. Stochastic Variational Inference (SVI) applies stochastic gradient descent to the ELBO, estimating the gradient from mini-batches rather than the full dataset. This makes VI applicable to datasets with millions of observations.

Because the ELBO factorises over data points (under a mean-field assumption with local latent variables), the gradient with respect to global parameters θ can be estimated from a mini-batch of size M:
The N/M scaling factor corrects for the fact that each mini-batch covers only M of the N data points. The estimator is unbiased when the mini-batch is sampled uniformly.
SVI distinguishes two kinds of latent variables:
zᵢ — one per data point (e.g. the latent code for observation xᵢ in a VAE). Their variational parameters φᵢ can be updated from the single corresponding observation.
β — shared across all data points (e.g. topic distributions in an LDA model). Their variational parameters require aggregation over the full dataset, which SVI approximates via mini-batch scaling.
The standard SVI update for a model with global parameter θ and per-point latent zᵢ:
Sample a mini-batch B ⊂ {1, …, N} of size M.
For each i ∈ B, optimise the local variational parameters φᵢ to convergence (or one step) holding θ fixed.
Compute the scaled mini-batch gradient ∇θ L ≈ (N/M) Σ_{i∈B} ∇θ Lᵢ.
Update θ with an SGD-style step (Adam, RMSProp, etc.).

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

class SVITrainer:
    """
    Minimal SVI trainer for a VAE-style model with amortised local inference.
    The encoder plays the role of the local variational parameters.
    """
    def __init__(self, vae, dataset, batch_size=128, lr=1e-3):
        self.vae    = vae
        self.N      = len(dataset)               # total dataset size
        self.loader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True)
        self.M      = batch_size
        self.optim  = torch.optim.Adam(vae.parameters(), lr=lr)

    def elbo_minibatch(self, x):
        """ELBO for one mini-batch, scaled to full-dataset equivalent."""
        x_recon, mu, log_var = self.vae(x)
        recon = self.vae.recon_loss(x_recon, x)         # E_q[log p(x|z)]
        kl    = -0.5 * (1 + log_var - mu.pow(2) - log_var.exp()).sum(dim=1).mean()
        # Scale: each mini-batch is an estimate of the full-data ELBO
        scale = self.N / len(x)              # N/M correction
        return scale * recon + kl            # global structure penalised at scale N

    def step(self, x):
        self.optim.zero_grad()
        loss = self.elbo_minibatch(x)
        loss.backward()                      # stochastic gradient of ELBO
        self.optim.step()
        return loss.item()

For exponential-family variational distributions, the natural gradient of the ELBO — the gradient in the space of sufficient statistics, preconditioned by the Fisher information matrix — has a particularly simple closed form. Natural gradient SVI converges faster than standard gradient SVI because it accounts for the curvature of the parameter space.

where F is the Fisher information matrix of q(z; λ) and λ are the natural parameters. For a Gaussian q, the natural gradient update for the mean λ₁ = Σ⁻¹μ and precision λ₂ = -½Σ⁻¹ simplifies to a coordinate update that resembles an EM step. In practice, Adam's adaptive learning rates approximate some of this preconditioning without requiring an explicit Fisher computation.

# Pyro's SVI interface encapsulates the SVI loop cleanly.
# pip install pyro-ppl
import pyro
import pyro.distributions as dist
from pyro.infer import SVI, Trace_ELBO
from pyro.optim import Adam

def model(data):
    """Generative model: z ~ N(0,1), x ~ N(z, 0.1)."""
    z = pyro.sample("z", dist.Normal(0., 1.).expand([data.shape[0]]).to_event(1))
    with pyro.plate("data", data.shape[0]):
        pyro.sample("x", dist.Normal(z, 0.1), obs=data)

def guide(data):
    """Variational distribution: q(z|x) = N(mu_q, sigma_q)."""
    mu_q    = pyro.param("mu_q",    torch.zeros(data.shape[0]))
    sigma_q = pyro.param("sigma_q", torch.ones(data.shape[0]),
                         constraint=dist.constraints.positive)
    pyro.sample("z", dist.Normal(mu_q, sigma_q).to_event(1))

svi = SVI(model, guide, Adam({"lr": 0.01}), loss=Trace_ELBO())
data = torch.randn(200, 10)
for step in range(500):
    loss = svi.step(data)
    if step % 100 == 0:
        print(f"Step {step}: ELBO loss = {loss:.2f}")
Mini-batch gradient estimates add noise. Two standard tools reduce variance:
replacing z ~ q(z|x) with z = g(φ, ε) gives a pathwise gradient estimate that is orders of magnitude lower variance than the score function (REINFORCE) estimator.
subtracting a baseline b from the reward f(z) in the score function estimator leaves the gradient unbiased while reducing variance. Common baselines are the sample mean or a learned neural baseline.
variance scales as 1/M. Doubling M halves the variance but also doubles the compute per step. The ideal M balances gradient variance against throughput.

SVI introduces three sources of stochasticity that classical VI avoids: mini-batch sampling, latent variable sampling (reparametrization), and local variable optimization quality. Each adds variance to the gradient estimate.

The N/M scaling amplifies per-sample ELBO fluctuations by N/M. For N=1M, M=256, this factor is ~4000, meaning noise in the mini-batch is amplified 4000× in the gradient estimate. Larger batches reduce this at the cost of compute.
Convergence slower than natural gradient SVI for conjugate models:

Standard gradient SVI ignores the curvature of the parameter space, requiring many more iterations than natural gradient SVI for exponential-family models where the natural gradient is available in closed form.

The reparametrization trick (low-variance gradient estimator) only applies when q(z; φ) = g(φ, ε) for ε ~ p(ε) — this holds for Gaussian, Laplace, and some other families, but not for discrete distributions without relaxations (Gumbel-Softmax, etc.).
For models with true local variables (not amortized), SVI requires optimizing φᵢ for each mini-batch element i, which may require multiple inner-loop iterations and adds overhead.
ELBO is not the true objective:

ELBO is a lower bound on log p(x). Maximizing ELBO does not guarantee that q(z; λ) closely matches the true posterior p(z | x) — it depends on the expressiveness of the chosen variational family. Mean-field approximations in particular underestimate posterior variance.
Your dataset has more than ~10,000 observations:
Classical batch VI is intractable at this scale. SVI is the standard approach.
You are training a VAE or deep generative model:
The ELBO objective for VAEs is an SVI objective. Training a VAE with Adam is SVI with amortized inference and reparametrization gradients.
You need uncertainty estimates at scale:
SVI provides approximate posteriors over parameters/latents, giving calibrated uncertainty estimates that maximum likelihood cannot.
Your model has exchangeable (i.i.d.) data points:
The mini-batch scaling derivation assumes data points are exchangeable. Topic models, VAEs, and most density estimation models satisfy this.
Time-series or structured data with non-exchangeable observations:
If p(x₁, ..., xₙ) ≠ p(x_{π(1)}, ..., x_{π(n)}) for permutations π, the N/M scaling is invalid. Use sequential VI or MCMC instead.
When you need exact posterior samples:
SVI produces an approximate posterior. If exact uncertainty quantification is required (e.g., safety-critical systems), use MCMC (HMC, NUTS) despite its higher cost.
If you do not need uncertainty estimates, maximum likelihood via standard backprop is simpler and faster. Use SVI only when the posterior distribution (not just its mode) is valuable.
With N < 1000 observations, batch VI or even MCMC is practical and more accurate. SVI's mini-batch approximation adds unnecessary noise for small N.
Real-World Example: Variational Autoencoder for Image Generation

A Variational Autoencoder is the canonical application of SVI with amortized inference. The encoder computes the variational parameters φᵢ = (μᵢ, σᵢ) for each input xᵢ; the decoder parametrizes the likelihood p(x | z). Training maximizes the ELBO via mini-batch SVI.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torchvision import datasets, transforms
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

class Encoder(nn.Module):
    """Amortized variational inference: q(z|x) = N(mu(x), diag(sigma(x)^2))"""
    def __init__(self, input_dim=784, hidden=256, latent=32):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden),    nn.ReLU(),
        )
        self.mu_head      = nn.Linear(hidden, latent)
        self.log_var_head = nn.Linear(hidden, latent)

    def forward(self, x):
        h = self.net(x.view(x.size(0), -1))
        return self.mu_head(h), self.log_var_head(h)

class Decoder(nn.Module):
    """p(x|z) = Bernoulli(f(z)) — for binary-ish images"""
    def __init__(self, latent=32, hidden=256, output_dim=784):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(latent, hidden),     nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden),     nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, output_dim), nn.Sigmoid(),
        )
    def forward(self, z):
        return self.net(z)

class VAE(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, latent=32):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = Encoder(latent=latent)
        self.decoder = Decoder(latent=latent)

    def reparametrize(self, mu, log_var):
        """Reparametrization trick: z = mu + sigma * eps, eps ~ N(0,I)"""
        std = (0.5 * log_var).exp()
        eps = torch.randn_like(std)
        return mu + std * eps   # pathwise gradient flows through mu and std

    def elbo(self, x):
        """
        ELBO = E_q[log p(x|z)] - KL(q(z|x) || p(z))
        KL has closed form for Gaussians:
        KL = -0.5 * sum(1 + log_var - mu^2 - exp(log_var))
        """
        mu, log_var = self.encoder(x)
        z          = self.reparametrize(mu, log_var)
        x_recon    = self.decoder(z)

        # Reconstruction: E_q[log p(x|z)] — binary cross-entropy
        recon_loss = F.binary_cross_entropy(
            x_recon, x.view(x.size(0), -1), reduction='sum'
        )

        # KL divergence (closed form): -0.5 * sum(1 + log_var - mu^2 - var)
        kl_div = -0.5 * (1 + log_var - mu.pow(2) - log_var.exp()).sum()

        return recon_loss + kl_div    # minimize negative ELBO

# Training loop — this IS SVI with amortized inference
vae    = VAE(latent=32)
optim  = torch.optim.Adam(vae.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loader = DataLoader(
    datasets.MNIST('/tmp/mnist', train=True, download=True,
                   transform=transforms.ToTensor()),
    batch_size=128, shuffle=True
)

for epoch in range(10):
    total_elbo = 0
    for x, _ in loader:
        optim.zero_grad()
        loss = vae.elbo(x)             # SVI step: mini-batch ELBO
        loss.backward()                # ∇_φ ELBO via reparametrization
        optim.step()
        total_elbo += loss.item()
    print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}: avg ELBO = {total_elbo / len(loader):.1f}")
In this setup, each call to

with a mini-batch of 128 images is one SVI step. The encoder network replaces per-datapoint variational parameters with a shared amortized function — a major practical advantage over classical mean-field VI. The reparametrization trick provides low-variance gradients through the sampling operation.

# WRONG: no dataset-size scaling — ELBO estimated on batch only
def elbo_wrong(x, model, guide):
    log_p = model(x)
    log_q = guide(x)
    return (log_p - log_q).mean()   # treats M observations as all of data

# CORRECT: scale the data log-likelihood term by N/M
N = len(dataset)
def elbo_correct(x, model, guide, N):
    log_q = guide.log_prob(x)
    log_p_z = prior.log_prob(guide.z)   # prior: does not scale
    log_p_x_given_z = likelihood.log_prob(x)   # scales with data
    return log_p_z + (N / len(x)) * log_p_x_given_z - log_q
2. Using score function estimator when reparametrization is available

The REINFORCE (score function) gradient estimator has variance 10–100× higher than the reparametrization estimator for continuous latent variables. Always use reparametrization for Gaussian, Laplace, or exponential variational distributions. Reserve REINFORCE for discrete latent variables where reparametrization is unavailable.

In mean-field VI, q(z) = ∏ᵢ qᵢ(zᵢ), the model assumes all latent variables are independent. For a VAE with many latent dimensions, some dimensions may 'collapse' (posterior matches prior exactly, KL=0, dimension unused). Signs include KL terms summing to near zero for some latent dimensions. Fix with KL annealing (gradually increase KL weight from 0 to 1 during training) or free bits (set a minimum KL per dimension).

# KL annealing: beta-VAE style, avoids posterior collapse
def elbo_annealed(x, encoder, decoder, beta=0.0, beta_max=1.0, step=0, warmup=5000):
    beta = min(beta_max, beta_max * step / warmup)
    mu, log_var = encoder(x)
    z = reparametrize(mu, log_var)
    recon = reconstruction_loss(decoder(z), x)
    kl    = -0.5 * (1 + log_var - mu.pow(2) - log_var.exp()).sum()
    return recon + beta * kl   # beta=0 at start, 1.0 after warmup steps
Setting eps=1.0 in Adam for SVI:
Adam with its default eps=1e-8 can become unstable when ELBO gradients are very noisy at the start of training. Use eps=1e-4 or 1e-3 for SVI training, especially for VAEs.

In natural gradient SVI for conjugate models, ELBO should monotonically increase. If it decreases, the step size is too large. In stochastic gradient SVI, ELBO is non-monotonic but should trend upward on average.
Start with amortized inference for deep models:
Use an encoder network (inference network) rather than per-datapoint variational parameters. Amortized inference generalizes to unseen data points and is standard in VAEs.
Use Adam with learning rate 1e-3 to 1e-4:
SVI with reparametrization and Adam is a well-tested combination. Start with lr=1e-3 and decay by 10× if ELBO plateaus.
Start with beta=0 (pure reconstruction) and linearly increase to beta=1 over 5,000–10,000 steps. This prevents posterior collapse and produces more disentangled representations.
Monitor each latent dimension's KL separately:
per training step. Dimensions with KL near 0 have collapsed — act early if this happens.
Use Pyro or NumPyro for complex models:
For models beyond VAEs (hierarchical Bayes, mixture models, topic models), use a probabilistic programming library that handles the SVI scaling and gradient computation automatically.
After training, evaluate the importance-weighted lower bound (IWAE) with K=100–1000 samples. IWELBO is tighter than ELBO and gives a better estimate of true log p(x).

# Importance-weighted ELBO (IWAE) for model evaluation
def iwelbo(x, encoder, decoder, K=100):
    """
    IWAE = E[log (1/K sum_k w_k)], w_k = p(x,z_k) / q(z_k|x)
    Tighter lower bound than ELBO; use for evaluation, not training.
    """
    mu, log_var = encoder(x)
    # Expand for K samples
    mu      = mu.unsqueeze(1).expand(-1, K, -1)      # (B, K, latent)
    log_var = log_var.unsqueeze(1).expand(-1, K, -1)
    eps     = torch.randn_like(mu)
    z       = mu + (0.5 * log_var).exp() * eps        # (B, K, latent)

    # log p(x|z)
    x_flat  = x.view(x.size(0), 1, -1).expand(-1, K, -1)
    x_recon = decoder(z.view(-1, z.size(-1))).view(*z.shape[:2], -1)
    log_p_x_given_z = -F.binary_cross_entropy(x_recon, x_flat, reduction='none').sum(-1)

    # log p(z) = N(0, I)
    log_p_z = -0.5 * (z.pow(2) + torch.log(torch.tensor(2 * 3.14159))).sum(-1)

    # log q(z|x) = N(mu, sigma^2)
    log_q_z = -0.5 * (((z - mu) / (0.5 * log_var).exp().add(1e-8)).pow(2)
                       + log_var + torch.log(torch.tensor(2 * 3.14159))).sum(-1)

    log_w = log_p_x_given_z + log_p_z - log_q_z   # (B, K)
    return torch.logsumexp(log_w, dim=1).mean() - torch.log(torch.tensor(float(K)))

SVI's computational complexity per update is O(M × model_cost), where M is the mini-batch size and model_cost is the cost of a single forward-backward pass through the generative model and inference network. For a VAE with encoder/decoder network of width H and latent dimension Z, model_cost ≈ O(H² + HZ). The total training cost to convergence is empirically O((N/M) × convergence_steps × M × model_cost) = O(N × convergence_steps × model_cost), which matches batch VI's cost per-epoch but with SGD's faster convergence (fewer epochs to convergence).

In practice, SVI is 10–100× faster than batch VI for large N. For N=1M documents and M=256, each SVI step processes 0.025% of the data, enabling 4000 parameter updates per epoch versus one for batch VI. Since gradient descent converges in O(1/lr) steps regardless of N (for well-tuned learning rates), SVI achieves full convergence in O(1/lr) steps while batch VI requires O(N/lr) operations per step — an N× speedup.

attrs.latex: \text{SVI wall-clock time} \propto \frac{\text{convergence steps}}{M} \cdot M \cdot C_\text{model} = \text{steps} \cdot C_\text{model}

content.text: Parallelization scales well: mini-batches are trivially data-parallel across GPUs. The gradient of the ELBO within a mini-batch is averaged across samples (before the N/M scaling), so standard data parallelism applies. For very large models with billions of parameters, model parallelism techniques from standard deep learning transfer directly.
Python probabilistic programming library built on PyTorch.
provides a clean interface: define model and guide, call
in a loop. Supports stochastic plates for mini-batch scaling.
JAX-based variant of Pyro. Supports JIT compilation and vectorized models for much faster inference than Pyro for medium-sized models. Recommended for non-GPU workflows.
runs SVI automatically given a model and surrogate family.
Gaussian process library that uses SVI (SVGP) for scalable GP inference. Uses inducing points to approximate the full GP posterior.
context manager handles the N/M mini-batch scaling automatically. Data inside a plate is treated as exchangeable and scaled correctly.
JAX optimizer library compatible with NumPyro. Adam, RMSProp, and gradient clipping all work out-of-the-box with SVI objectives in JAX.

# NumPyro SVI example: Bayesian linear regression
import numpyro
import numpyro.distributions as dist
from numpyro.infer import SVI, Trace_ELBO
from numpyro.infer.autoguide import AutoNormal
import optax
import jax.numpy as jnp
import jax

N, D = 10000, 20
X = jax.random.normal(jax.random.PRNGKey(0), (N, D))
y = X @ jnp.ones(D) + jax.random.normal(jax.random.PRNGKey(1), (N,)) * 0.5

def model(X, y):
    """Bayesian linear regression with Gaussian prior."""
    w     = numpyro.sample("w", dist.Normal(0, 1).expand([D]))
    sigma = numpyro.sample("sigma", dist.HalfNormal(1.0))
    with numpyro.plate("data", N, subsample_size=128) as idx:
        numpyro.sample("y", dist.Normal(X[idx] @ w, sigma), obs=y[idx])
    # plate handles N/M scaling automatically

guide = AutoNormal(model)   # mean-field Gaussian variational family
optimizer = numpyro.optim.optax_to_numpyro(optax.adam(1e-3))
svi   = SVI(model, guide, optimizer, loss=Trace_ELBO())
state = svi.init(jax.random.PRNGKey(2), X, y)

for step in range(2000):
    state, loss = svi.update(state, X, y)
    if step % 200 == 0:
        print(f"Step {step}: ELBO = {-loss:.1f}")

params = svi.get_params(state)
print("Posterior mean w:", guide.median(params)["w"][:5])

The key trade-off in choosing an inference algorithm is speed vs. accuracy of uncertainty quantification. MAP provides no uncertainty. Dropout-based Bayes approximations are cheap but poorly calibrated. SVI provides approximate posteriors that are well-calibrated for well-specified models and is the best choice when scalability and calibrated uncertainty are both required. MCMC provides asymptotically exact posteriors but is typically 100–1000× more expensive per effectively independent sample.

In practice, posterior collapse (KL → 0 for some dimensions) is the most common failure mode. It causes the model to ignore parts of the latent space, reducing expressivity. Monitor per-dimension KL during training and apply KL annealing or free-bits regularization.

Powerful decoders can achieve high ELBO by ignoring z entirely (the decoder learns the data distribution directly). Signs: reconstruction loss is very low, KL is very low. Fix: use weaker decoders, add explicit capacity constraints, or use a structured latent space.
If mini-batches are not sampled uniformly (e.g., sequential ordering in a streaming setting), the N/M scaling is biased. Always shuffle or use reservoir sampling for truly random mini-batches.
Computing the ELBO involves log E[p/q], which can underflow/overflow in float32. Always use numerically stable implementations:

The encoder network can overfit to the training distribution, producing overconfident posteriors on test data. Regularize the encoder with dropout or use cross-validation to monitor posterior predictive performance on held-out data.
applies stochastic gradient descent to the ELBO by estimating full-data gradients from mini-batches scaled by N/M — making variational inference feasible for large datasets.
corrects for mini-batch subsampling when the data log-likelihood factorizes over observations. Omitting this factor is a common implementation error.
(shared) receive different treatment: local updates only need the corresponding observation; global updates need the scaled mini-batch gradient.
(z = μ + σε, ε ~ N(0,I)) reduces gradient variance by 10–100× versus the score function estimator, and is the default for continuous latent variables.
VAE is SVI with amortized inference
: the encoder network parametrizes the variational distribution q(z|x), eliminating per-datapoint parameter optimization.
(KL → 0 for some latent dimensions) — apply KL annealing (start with β=0, anneal to β=1) or free-bits to prevent it.
for complex models — they handle N/M scaling, the reparametrization trick, and optimizer integration automatically, reducing implementation errors.
using K=100+ samples for a tighter lower bound than the training ELBO.
